title: Synthesis precursors
content: The starting materials used for the preparation of superconducting samples in the Tl–Ca/Ba–Cu–O system are thallium(III) oxide ($Tl_{2}O_{3}$), calcium oxide ($CaO$), and a barium copper oxide precursor ($BaCu_{3}O_{4}$).

extra.notations:
- $Tl_{2}O_{3}$: Thallium(III) oxide
- $CaO$: Calcium oxide
- $BaCu_{3}O_{4}$: Barium copper oxide precursor
- $T_{c}$: Superconducting transition temperature

title: Properties of BaCu3O4 precursor
content: In the synthesis of Tl-based superconductors, $BaCu_{3}O_{4}$ is utilized as a precursor because it possesses the lowest melting point among the various barium-copper oxides ($Ba$-$Cu$-$O$); furthermore, molten $BaCu_{3}O_{4}$ exhibits high fluidity, which promotes more complete melt-solid reactions during synthesis compared to standard solid-state reactions.

extra.notations:
- $Tl_{2}O_{3}$: Thallium(III) oxide
- $CaO$: Calcium oxide
- $BaCu_{3}O_{4}$: Barium copper oxide precursor
- $T_{c}$: Superconducting transition temperature

title: Synthesis procedure
content: The procedure for synthesizing bulk Tl-Ca/Ba-Cu-O superconductors involves mixing stoichiometric amounts of $Tl_{2}O_{3}$, $CaO$, and $BaCu_{3}O_{4}$ precursors, grinding and pressing the mixture into a pellet, heating said pellet to a temperature between 880 °C and 910 °C for a duration of 3 to 5 minutes in a flowing oxygen environment, and subsequently quenching the sample to room temperature in air.

extra.notations:
- $Tl_{2}O_{3}$: Thallium(III) oxide
- $CaO$: Calcium oxide
- $BaCu_{3}O_{4}$: Barium copper oxide precursor
- $T_{c}$: Superconducting transition temperature

title: Measurement technique
content: The electrical resistance of the synthesized Tl-Ca/Ba-Cu-O superconducting samples is measured as a function of temperature using the standard four-probe technique applied to rectangular bars cut from the prepared pellets, with electrical contacts established using silver paste.

extra.notations:
- $Tl_{2}O_{3}$: Thallium(III) oxide
- $CaO$: Calcium oxide
- $BaCu_{3}O_{4}$: Barium copper oxide precursor
- $T_{c}$: Superconducting transition temperature

title: Definition of onset temperature
content: The superconducting onset temperature, denoted as the temperature at which the transition to superconductivity begins, is defined and estimated as the specific temperature at which the curvature in the electrical resistance versus temperature ($R$ vs. $T$) plot exhibits the most rapid change.

extra.notations:
- $Tl_{2}O_{3}$: Thallium(III) oxide
- $CaO$: Calcium oxide
- $BaCu_{3}O_{4}$: Barium copper oxide precursor
- $T_{c}$: Superconducting transition temperature

title: Magnetic indicators of superconductivity
content: In the Tl-Ca/Ba-Cu-O system, the presence of superconductivity is verified qualitatively by observing strong diamagnetic repulsion, characterized by the levitation of the sample over a magnetic field (Meissner effect), and is quantitatively characterized by measuring the temperature dependence of the alternating current (a.c.) magnetic susceptibility.

extra.notations:
- $Tl_{2}O_{3}$: Thallium(III) oxide
- $CaO$: Calcium oxide
- $BaCu_{3}O_{4}$: Barium copper oxide precursor
- $T_{c}$: Superconducting transition temperature

title: Discovery of High-Tc Superconductivity in the Tl–Ca/Ba–Cu–O System
content: Stable and reproducible bulk superconductivity is established in the Tl–Ca/Ba–Cu–O system, characterized by an onset temperature of 120 K (determined by the rapid change in curvature of the resistance-temperature plot) and zero resistance achieved above 100 K.

extra.notations:
- $Tl_{2}O_{3}$: Thallium(III) oxide
- $CaO$: Calcium oxide
- $BaCu_{3}O_{4}$: Barium copper oxide precursor
- $T_{c}$: Superconducting transition temperature

title: Superconductivity of Tl-Ba-Cu-O
content: Samples in the Tl-Ba-Cu-O chemical system exhibit bulk superconductivity characterized by zero electrical resistance at temperatures just above the boiling point of liquid nitrogen ($77$ K).

extra.notations:
- $T_c$: Superconducting transition temperature (critical temperature).
- Tl-Ba-Cu-O: Thallium-Barium-Copper-Oxygen chemical system.
- Tl-Ca/Ba-Cu-O: Thallium-Calcium/Barium-Copper-Oxygen chemical system.
- Tl-Ca-Cu-O: Thallium-Calcium-Copper-Oxygen chemical system.

title: Effect of Annealing on Tl-Ba-Cu-O
content: In the Tl-Ba-Cu-O superconducting system, the process of annealing samples at $450^{\circ}\text{C}$ in an environment of flowing oxygen results in the depression or complete destruction of the superconducting transition.

extra.notations:
- $T_c$: Superconducting transition temperature (critical temperature).
- Tl-Ba-Cu-O: Thallium-Barium-Copper-Oxygen chemical system.
- Tl-Ca/Ba-Cu-O: Thallium-Calcium/Barium-Copper-Oxygen chemical system.
- Tl-Ca-Cu-O: Thallium-Calcium-Copper-Oxygen chemical system.

title: Effect of Annealing on Tl-Ca/Ba-Cu-O
content: In the Tl-Ca/Ba-Cu-O superconducting system, the process of annealing samples at $450^{\circ}\text{C}$ in an environment of flowing oxygen results in a slight improvement of the superconducting properties, such as the transition temperature or resistance profile.

extra.notations:
- $T_c$: Superconducting transition temperature (critical temperature).
- Tl-Ba-Cu-O: Thallium-Barium-Copper-Oxygen chemical system.
- Tl-Ca/Ba-Cu-O: Thallium-Calcium/Barium-Copper-Oxygen chemical system.
- Tl-Ca-Cu-O: Thallium-Calcium-Copper-Oxygen chemical system.

title: Conductivity of Tl-Ca-Cu-O
content: Samples in the Tl-Ca-Cu-O chemical system, when prepared using thermal and atmospheric conditions similar to those used for the Tl-Ca/Ba-Cu-O system (such as heating to $880$–$910^{\circ}\text{C}$ in flowing oxygen), exhibit semiconductor-like electrical resistivity behavior down to the temperature of liquid nitrogen ($77$ K) rather than metallic or superconducting behavior.

extra.notations:
- $T_c$: Superconducting transition temperature (critical temperature).
- Tl-Ba-Cu-O: Thallium-Barium-Copper-Oxygen chemical system.
- Tl-Ca/Ba-Cu-O: Thallium-Calcium/Barium-Copper-Oxygen chemical system.
- Tl-Ca-Cu-O: Thallium-Calcium-Copper-Oxygen chemical system.

title: Compositional and Thermal Stability Characteristics
content: The addition of calcium to the Tl-Ba-Cu-O system stabilizes the superconducting phase against annealing; annealing at $450^{\circ}C$ in flowing oxygen slightly improves the superconductivity of Tl–Ca/Ba–Cu–O samples, whereas it depresses or destroys the transition in Tl–Ba–Cu–O samples, and Tl–Ca–Cu–O samples without barium exhibit semiconductor-like behavior down to liquid-nitrogen temperatures.

extra.notations:
- $T_c$: Superconducting transition temperature (critical temperature).
- Tl-Ba-Cu-O: Thallium-Barium-Copper-Oxygen chemical system.
- Tl-Ca/Ba-Cu-O: Thallium-Calcium/Barium-Copper-Oxygen chemical system.
- Tl-Ca-Cu-O: Thallium-Calcium-Copper-Oxygen chemical system.

title: Theoretical Prediction of YH10
content: Theoretical calculations predict a face-centred cubic (fcc) yttrium hydride phase with the composition $YH_{10}$ that possesses a superconducting critical temperature $T_{c}$ estimated to be 303 K at a pressure of 400 GPa or between 305 K and 326 K at a pressure of 250 GPa.

extra.notations:
- $T_{c}$: superconducting critical temperature
- $P$: pressure
- $M$: atomic mass
- $\alpha$: isotope effect coefficient
- $\xi$: coherence length of the superconducting order parameter
- $\phi_{0}$: magnetic flux quantum
- $H_{c2}$: upper critical field
- hcp: hexagonal close-packed crystal structure
- fcc: face-centred cubic crystal structure
- bcc: body-centred cubic crystal structure
- $P6_{3}/mmc$: space group identifier for a hexagonal crystal system
- $Im\bar{3}m$: space group identifier for a body-centred cubic crystal system

title: Predicted Stability of fcc-YH10 at High Pressure
content: Theoretical $ab$ $initio$ calculations predict that the face-centred cubic ($fcc$) phase of yttrium decahydride ($YH_{10}$) reaches thermodynamic stability at an applied pressure of 250 GPa.

extra.notations:
- $fcc$: face-centred cubic
- $hcp$: hexagonal close packed
- $YH_{x}$: yttrium hydride with hydrogen-to-yttrium ratio $x$
- $P6_{3}/mmc$: space group symmetry identifier for a specific hexagonal crystal structure
- $G$: Gibbs free energy
- $T$: temperature
- $P$: pressure

title: Predicted High-Temperature Stability of fcc-YH10
content: Theoretical $ab$ $initio$ calculations suggest that although yttrium decahydride ($YH_{10}$) may be thermodynamically unstable at 0 K, the difference in Gibbs free energies ($\Delta G$) between the face-centred cubic ($fcc$) $YH_{10}$ phase and the hexagonal close packed ($hcp$) $YH_{9}$ phase decreases as temperature increases, such that $fcc-YH_{10}$ is predicted to become more thermodynamically favourable than $hcp-YH_{9}$ at temperatures exceeding 1500 K at a pressure of 375 GPa, or at temperatures exceeding 1100 K at a pressure of 400 GPa.

extra.notations:
- $fcc$: face-centred cubic
- $hcp$: hexagonal close packed
- $YH_{x}$: yttrium hydride with hydrogen-to-yttrium ratio $x$
- $P6_{3}/mmc$: space group symmetry identifier for a specific hexagonal crystal structure
- $G$: Gibbs free energy
- $T$: temperature
- $P$: pressure

title: Criterion for Excess Hydrogen Availability
content: In high-pressure chemical synthesis experiments using ammonia borane ($NH_{3}BH_{3}$) as a hydrogen source and yttrium trihydride ($YH_{3}$) as a reactant, the successful realization of an excess hydrogen ($H_2$) environment is verified if the initial $YH_{3}$ phase undergoes a complete structural transformation into a hydrogen-rich, single-phase $YH_{9}$ with $P6_{3}/mmc$ symmetry following laser heating.

extra.notations:
- $fcc$: face-centred cubic
- $hcp$: hexagonal close packed
- $YH_{x}$: yttrium hydride with hydrogen-to-yttrium ratio $x$
- $P6_{3}/mmc$: space group symmetry identifier for a specific hexagonal crystal structure
- $G$: Gibbs free energy
- $T$: temperature
- $P$: pressure

title: Identification of Crystal Phases via Diffraction
content: The identification of specific yttrium hydride phases, such as the $P6_{3}/mmc$ $YH_{9}$ phase, is established by demonstrating structural agreement between experimental X-ray powder diffraction patterns and calculated diffraction peak positions derived from theoretical crystal structure models.

extra.notations:
- $fcc$: face-centred cubic
- $hcp$: hexagonal close packed
- $YH_{x}$: yttrium hydride with hydrogen-to-yttrium ratio $x$
- $P6_{3}/mmc$: space group symmetry identifier for a specific hexagonal crystal structure
- $G$: Gibbs free energy
- $T$: temperature
- $P$: pressure

title: Absence of predicted fcc-YH10 phase
content: Contrary to theoretical predictions suggesting the stability of a high-$T_{c}$ face-centered cubic (fcc) $YH_{10}$ phase, the authors conclude that $YH_{10}$ does not form in the pressure-temperature range up to 410 GPa and 2250 K. Instead, the authors establish that the $P6_3/mmc$ $YH_{9}$ phase remains the hydrogen-richest yttrium hydride stable under these conditions.

extra.notations:
- $fcc$: face-centred cubic
- $hcp$: hexagonal close packed
- $YH_{x}$: yttrium hydride with hydrogen-to-yttrium ratio $x$
- $P6_{3}/mmc$: space group symmetry identifier for a specific hexagonal crystal structure
- $G$: Gibbs free energy
- $T$: temperature
- $P$: pressure

title: Critical role of oxygen annealing
content: Post-synthesis annealing in flowing oxygen at $300^{\circ}C$ is required to maximize the transition temperature in the Hg-Ba-Ca-Cu-O system; as-sintered samples exhibit a significantly lower maximum $T_{c}$ of $\sim 117$ K compared to the $\sim 133$ K achieved after annealing.

extra.notations:
- $Hg-1201$: $HgBa_{2}CuO_{4+\delta}$
- $Tl-1201$: $TlBa_{2}CuO_{5}$
- $Hg-1212$: $HgBa_{2}CaCu_{2}O_{6+x}$
- $Hg-1223$: $HgBa_{2}Ca_{2}Cu_{3}O_{8+x}$
- $T_{c}$: superconducting transition temperature
- $\rho$: average mass density
- $n$: number of $CuO_{2}$ layers per unit cell

title: Oxygen annealing modifies Tc in Hg- and Tl-based cuprates
content: Post‑synthesis annealing in a flowing oxygen atmosphere can modify the superconducting transition temperature $T_{c}$ and related resistive transition properties in layered copper‑oxide (cuprate) superconductors. Here "post‑synthesis annealing" denotes heating a synthesized cuprate sample in a continuous oxygen (O$_2$) gas flow at an elevated temperature for a finite time; "modifies" means the treatment changes the measured $T_{c}$ or the shape/width of the resistive superconducting transition, without specifying the direction (increase or decrease), which depends on the particular compound, annealing temperature, and processing history.

extra.notations:
- $T_{c}$: superconducting critical temperature (Kelvin), the temperature at which the sample shows a superconducting transition in resistivity or susceptibility.
- "Post‑synthesis annealing in flowing oxygen": heating a synthesized oxide sample in a continuous O$_2$ atmosphere (as opposed to inert or static atmosphere) at an elevated temperature for a processing interval.
- "Cuprate superconductors": layered copper‑oxide materials containing CuO$_2$ planes, e.g., Hg‑Ba‑Ca‑Cu‑O and Tl‑Ba‑Cu‑O families.

title: Annealing at 450°C in O₂ alters superconducting transitions in Tl cuprates
content: Annealing Tl‑containing layered cuprate samples (Tl‑Ba‑Cu‑O / Tl‑Ca/Ba‑Cu‑O) at 450°C in a flowing oxygen atmosphere alters their superconducting transition temperature $T_{c}$ and/or the resistive transition.

refine_history.reasoning: The original parent combined opposing specific outcomes from the two children (one reports slight improvement, the other reports suppression/destruction), asserting that literature reports span both directions. That union claim is not entailed by either child individually (union error), and the edge was misclassified as a "contradiction_join" rather than a partial overlap. Both children do, however, individually entail the weaker, common claim that annealing at 450°C in flowing O₂ changes the superconducting transition (each reports a change, albeit in opposite directions). The rewrite removes any assertion about the direction or about divergent reports and keeps only the intersectional, self-contained factual claim that is supported by every child.

extra.notations:
- $T_{c}$: superconducting critical temperature (Kelvin).
      - "450°C in flowing oxygen": thermal treatment consisting of heating the sample to 450 degrees Celsius in a continuous oxygen gas flow.
      - "Tl‑Ba‑Cu‑O / Tl‑Ca/Ba‑Cu‑O": thallium‑based layered cuprate superconductor families.

title: Theoretical prediction of a high‑Tc fcc YH10 phase
content: Theoretical calculations predict a face-centered cubic (fcc) yttrium hydride with composition YH10 that is expected to have a high superconducting critical temperature.

refine_history.reasoning:
The original parent combined two different claims from different children: (a) detailed numerical theoretical predictions (specific $T_{c}$ estimates and pressure ranges) coming from Child 251, and (b) an experimental null result and phase identification (that YH10 does not form up to 410 GPa and 2250 K) coming from Child 269. Neither child individually entails that combined union. Verification flagged this as a union error and recommended the edge be classed as partial_overlap.

To satisfy the requirement that every child individually entails the parent, I removed any material supported by only one child (the explicit numerical $T_{c}$ estimates, the pressure values, and the experimental refutation/phase identification). The rewritten proposition states only the intersection: both children assert the existence of theoretical predictions proposing an fcc YH10 phase with high superconducting $T_{c}$. This revised parent is self-contained, factual, and is individually entailed by Child 251 (which gives the detailed theoretical prediction) and by Child 269 (which refers generically to theoretical predictions of a high‑$T_{c}$ fcc YH10 phase).

extra.notations:
- $YH_{10}$: yttrium hydride with hydrogen-to-yttrium ratio 10
      - fcc: face-centered cubic crystal structure
      - $T_{c}$: superconducting critical temperature

title: Optimal annealing temperature for Tc is compound‑dependent
content: The annealing temperature required to produce a beneficial modification of the superconducting transition is not universal across the cited cuprates: Hg‑based Hg‑1223 (and related Hg‑Ba‑Ca‑Cu‑O compounds) require post‑synthesis oxygen annealing at about $300^{\circ}$C to reach their reported maximum $T_{c}$, whereas Tl‑containing layered cuprates (Tl‑Ba‑Cu‑O / Tl‑Ca/Ba‑Cu‑O) are reported to show improvements after annealing at $450^{\circ}$C. Therefore, the temperature that produces the desired modification of $T_{c}$ depends on the specific cuprate compound/family.

extra.notations:
- $T_{c}$: superconducting critical temperature; temperature values in degrees Celsius.
- "Hg‑based": Hg‑Ba‑Ca‑Cu‑O family; "Tl‑containing": Tl‑Ba‑Cu‑O / Tl‑Ca/Ba‑Cu‑O families.

title: Oxygen uptake during annealing likely adjusts hole concentration and thereby alters Tc
content: Post‑synthesis annealing in flowing O$_2$ plausibly changes the oxygen non‑stoichiometry (denoted $x$ in formulas like HgBa$_{2}$Ca$_{2}$Cu$_{3}$O$_{8+x}$), which in turn modifies the hole concentration in the CuO$_2$ planes; changes in hole concentration commonly move cuprates along the superconducting dome and therefore alter the measured $T_{c}$ and the resistive transition shape/width. Thus the observed modifications of $T_{c}$ upon oxygen annealing are likely mediated by changes in oxygen content and resultant carrier (hole) doping.

extra.notations:
- O$_2$ annealing: heating in flowing oxygen gas.
- $x$: oxygen non‑stoichiometry parameter in formulas HgBa$_{2}$Ca$_{2}$Cu$_{3}$O$_{8+x}$, etc.
- "hole concentration": number of mobile holes per Cu in CuO$_2$ planes (doping level).
